user_input: What is plagiarism and how can I prevent it?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: What is plagiarism and how can I prevent it?

FAQ Answer:
Take a look at our library guide search results on Academic Integrity . It includes valuable information plus links to additional resources to help you! We also have tutorials about these topics: Academic Integrity Plagiarism Awareness', 'FAQ Question: Do you have any tutorials about citing sources?

FAQ Answer:
Need to learn about citing sources? Here is a list of tutorials designed by our experts to get you started... Academic Integrity APA Citation Style Citing Your Sources MLA Citation Style Plagiarism Awareness And we have more tutorials to help you! Tutorials web page']
response:
Plagiarism is when you use someone else’s ideas, words, or work without giving them proper credit. It’s considered a breach of academic integrity because it presents another person’s intellectual property as your own.

**How you can prevent plagiarism**

1. **Explore the Academic Integrity guide** – The ASU Library’s guide on Academic Integrity (search “Academic Integrity” in the library guide) provides a solid overview of what counts as plagiarism and why it matters.  

2. **Use the Plagiarism‑Awareness tutorial** – The library offers a specific “Plagiarism Awareness” tutorial that walks you through common pitfalls and shows you how to keep your work original.  

3. **Cite sources correctly** – While this FAQ doesn’t list the citation tutorials, the library also has tutorials on APA, MLA, and other citation styles that teach you how to give proper attribution.  

4. **Check your work with a plagiarism checker** – Many ASU resources (e.g., Turnitin) let you run a draft through a similarity report so you can spot any uncited material before you submit.  

5. **Ask a librarian for help** – If you’re unsure about whether something needs a citation or how to paraphrase correctly, you can always reach out through the “Ask a Librarian” service for personalized guidance.

By reviewing the Academic Integrity guide and completing the Plagiarism Awareness tutorial, you’ll get the tools you need to avoid accidental plagiarism and maintain the highest standards of scholarly honesty. If you need more detailed instructions or have a specific situation in mind, feel free to ask a librarian for further assistance.
reference: Take a look at our library guide search results on Academic Integrity . It includes valuable information plus links to additional resources to help you! We also have tutorials about these topics: Academic Integrity Plagiarism Awareness

user_input: Can I use Respondus Lockdown Browser on library computers?

['FAQ Question: Can I use Respondus Lockdown Browser on library computers?

FAQ Answer:
Yes, the Respondus Lockdown Browser is installed on all public library computers at all of our library locations, as well as on computers managed at ASU computing sites. To access it, you need to follow these instructions : In the Windows Search bar in the bottom left corner of the screen, search for "Lockdown" (note: searching for "Respondus" will not work.) Click on the link for "Lockdown Browser - Lab Edition" A "Choose Server" pop-up window will appear - select "Canvas at ASU" from the drop-down menu. Log in with your ASURITE ID and password when prompted. Your browser should now be locked and you should see your Canvas courses. Navigate to the appropriate course and quiz/test. To exit the Lockdown Browser, click on the red X in the top right hand corner and answer the prompt as appropriate. Please note that all applications, including Lockdown Browser, are managed by the University Technology Office, not ASU Library. If you have questions or are experiencing technical difficulties using the Lockdown Browser, please contact the UTO Experience Center .', 'FAQ Question: Technical support...details that help us troubleshoot your technical issues...

FAQ Answer:
When submitting a ticket\xa0about a technical problem with ASU Library resources , please include as much detail as possible. The following information is especially helpful: Are you at home, at work, or on campus when the difficulty occurs? Are you logged into your ASUrite account? What Operating System are you using? (Windows 8, macOS 10.12, etc.) What Browser and Version Number are you using? (Internet Explorer 11, Firefox 52.2.1esr, etc.) Description of the steps you followed and at which screen the problem or error message appeared What is the URL (http://...) at the point of the error? Exact wording of any error messages Any screenshots that you may think would be helpful A telephone number where you may be reached during business hours In the meantime, here are a few things to try: Switch to a different browser (one may work better than another for a particular database or application: Firefox, Chrome, Internet Explorer, etc.) If you are coming in through MyASU, try starting from the library home page https://lib.asu.edu and then make sure you are signed in at the top right corner of the library page.']
response:
Yes—you can use Respondus Lockdown Browser on any public library computer at ASU’s library locations (as well as on computers managed at other ASU computing sites).

**How to launch it**

1. **Find the program**  
   - In the Windows Search bar (lower‑left corner of the screen) type **“Lockdown.”**  
   - *Do not* search for “Respondus,” because that won’t return the correct shortcut.  

2. **Open the correct entry**  
   - Click the link labeled **“Lockdown Browser – Lab Edition.”**  

3. **Select your server**  
   - A “Choose Server” pop‑up will appear.  
   - From the drop‑down menu choose **“Canvas at ASU.”**  

4. **Log in**  
   - When prompted, enter your **ASURITE ID** and password.  

5. **Navigate to your course**  
   - The browser will lock and display your Canvas courses.  
   - Open the appropriate course and go to the quiz or test that requires Lockdown Browser.  

6. **Exiting**  
   - When you’re finished, click the **red X** in the top‑right corner and respond to the exit prompt as instructed.

**If you run into technical problems**

All applications on library computers—including Respondus Lockdown Browser—are managed by the University Technology Office (UTO), not the ASU Library. For assistance, contact the **UTO Experience Center**.

**Quick troubleshooting checklist (if you need to open a support ticket)**  

- Are you on campus, at work, or at home?  
- Are you logged into your ASURITE account?  
- What operating system are you using (e.g., Windows 10, macOS 12)?  
- Which browser and version are you using (if you need to switch browsers)?  
- Describe the steps you took and where the error occurred.  
- Provide the exact URL and any error‑message wording.  
- Include screenshots if possible.  
- Provide a phone number where you can be reached during business hours.

If you need further help, you can also submit a ticket through the **Ask a Librarian** portal or start from the library home page at https://lib.asu.edu and make sure you’re signed in at the top right.

user_input: My instructor wants us to use credible sources. How do I know what's good?
retrieved_contexts:
["FAQ Question: My instructor wants us to use credible sources. How do I know what's good?

FAQ Answer:
Many instructors want you to use factual information that appears in reputable scholarly journals or books rather than opinions or unreliable sources found online from an unknown party.\xa0 Most of our library databases let you limit your search to scholarly resources, but even those can include opinionated sections that may not be what your instructor wants. First, make sure you know what types of resources are acceptable for your assignment!\xa0 Sometimes newspaper articles are acceptable, but sometimes they are not! Book reviews in a scholarly journal may be ok, but maybe not! So check with your instructor or check your syllabus for specific resource types that are allowed or not allowed. Need to learn how to evaluate resources? Read the script or view the tutorial about Evaluating Resources!", 'FAQ Question: Do you have any tutorials about citing sources?

FAQ Answer:
Need to learn about citing sources? Here is a list of tutorials designed by our experts to get you started... Academic Integrity APA Citation Style Citing Your Sources MLA Citation Style Plagiarism Awareness And we have more tutorials to help you! Tutorials web page']
response:
When your instructor asks you to use **credible sources**, the goal is to base your work on reliable, scholarly information rather than personal opinions or unverified material you might find on the open web. Here’s a step‑by‑step guide to help you determine what’s “good” for your assignments:

### 1. Start with the Assignment Guidelines  
1. **Read the syllabus or assignment sheet carefully.**  
   - Look for any explicit statements about the types of sources that are allowed (e.g., “peer‑reviewed journal articles,” “books published by academic presses,” “newspaper articles,” etc.).  
2. **Ask your instructor if anything is unclear.**  
   - If you’re unsure whether a particular source type (like a book review or a newspaper editorial) is acceptable, a quick email or office‑hours question can save you time later.

### 2. Use the Library’s Scholarly Databases  
- **Limit your search to “scholarly” or “peer‑reviewed” materials.** Most of ASU Library’s databases have a filter you can turn on that restricts results to scholarly resources.  
- **Be aware that even scholarly journals can contain opinion pieces** (e.g., editorials, commentaries). If your instructor wants strictly research‑based evidence, look for articles labeled as “research articles,” “original studies,” or “review articles.”

### 3. Evaluate Each Source (Even When It’s Scholarly)  
The library offers a short tutorial on **Evaluating Resources** that walks you through the classic CRAAP test (Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose). Here’s a quick checklist you can apply:

| Criterion | What to Look For |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Currency** | Publication date—Is the information up‑to‑date for your topic? |
| **Relevance** | Does the source directly address your research question? |
| **Authority** | Who is the author? Are they an expert in the field (e.g., professor, researcher, professional organization)? |
| **Accuracy** | Are the claims supported by evidence, citations, or data? Is the methodology sound? |
| **Purpose** | Is the work intended to inform, persuade, sell, or entertain? Scholarly works aim to inform and advance knowledge. |

If a source checks most or all of these boxes, it’s likely a solid choice.

### 4. Commonly Accepted Credible Source Types  
- **Peer‑reviewed journal articles** (research reports, systematic reviews, meta‑analyses)  
- **Books and monographs** published by academic presses (e.g., Oxford University Press, Springer)  
- **Conference proceedings** from reputable scholarly societies  
- **Government publications** (e.g., reports from the CDC, EPA)  
- **Professional organization reports** (e.g., IEEE, American Psychological Association)  

### 5. Sources That May Need Extra Scrutiny  
- **Newspaper articles** – Often acceptable for current‑events context, but verify the author’s credentials and the newspaper’s reputation.  
- **Book reviews** – Useful for understanding scholarly reception, but they are not primary research.  
- **Editorials or opinion pieces** – Generally not suitable unless your assignment explicitly calls for multiple perspectives.  

### 6. Where to Find Tutorials & Help  
- **Evaluating Resources tutorial** – Walks you through the CRAAP test and gives examples of good vs. weak sources.  
- **Citing Sources tutorials** – Once you’ve selected credible material, you’ll also need to cite it correctly (APA, MLA, etc.). The library’s “Citing Your Sources” tutorial can guide you through that process.  

### 7. When You’re Still Unsure  
If after checking the guidelines, filters, and evaluation criteria you’re still not confident about a source, you can:

1. **Ask a librarian** via the “Ask a Librarian” service – they can quickly confirm whether a source meets your instructor’s expectations.  
2. **Consult a peer mentor** or writing center for a second opinion on source credibility.  

---

**Bottom line:**  
1. **Know what your instructor expects** (check the syllabus or ask).  
2. **Use the library’s scholarly databases and apply the “scholarly” filter.**  
3. **Evaluate each source with the CRAAP criteria** (or the library’s Evaluating Resources tutorial).  
4. **When in doubt, reach out to a librarian** for confirmation.

Good luck with your research, and feel free to let me know if you need help locating specific databases or navigating the evaluation tutorial!
reference: Many instructors want you to use factual information that appears in reputable scholarly journals or books rather than opinions or unreliable sources found online from an unknown party.  Most of our library databases let you limit your search to scholarly resources, but even those can include opinionated sections that may not be what your instructor wants. First, make sure you know what types of resources are acceptable for your assignment!  Sometimes newspaper articles are acceptable, but sometimes they are not! Book reviews in a scholarly journal may be ok, but maybe not! So check with your instructor or check your syllabus for specific resource types that are allowed or not allowed. Need to learn how to evaluate resources? Read the script or view the tutorial about Evaluating Resources!

user_input: How do I add people outside of ASU to manage my ASU Research Data Repository datasets?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: How do I add people outside of ASU to manage my ASU Research Data Repository datasets?

FAQ Answer:
With our current ASU Research Data Repository authentication process, only submitters with asu.edu emails can create an account. Anyone outside the university can view and access published collections (known as Dataverses), but\xa0cannot directly submit/deposit or manage the metadata. If you have members of your project outside of ASU, you can have them submit their datasets and documentation to a secure sharing space like Google Drive or Dropbox and then upload the files on their behalf. If more direct interaction is desired, ASU sponsors can request that collaborators get an “affiliate ID” (ASURITE) to access ASU systems. The ID expires after one year and has to be renewed by the ASU sponsor at that time. To sponsor and request an ASU ID for your collaborators, fill out the ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request - Add or Renew form . Additional workarounds for research teams may include using the Dataverse API , research notebooks like LabArchives , or the OSF as a collaboration platform, and connecting the OSF to Dataverse (which uses an API token). For information on adding co-owners, see our FAQ entry on adding users or co-owners to my datasets .', 'FAQ Question: How do I add users or co-owners to my datasets in the ASU Research Data Repository?

FAQ Answer:
We are using ASU single sign-on as the only method for signing in, and users add their accounts. Library admins cannot add them before they sign in for the first time, and users have read-only access when they are initially added. ASU Library staff provide permissions. Please contact the library and notify us which user you need added, after they have signed into the repository explorer page for the first time. Accounts are created at the time of first sign-in, and after that, the library repository administration team can add them to the account with the appropriate permission levels and even assign them to groups. If you only want\xa0to list someone\xa0as an author, but not grant permissions to manage your datasets, add their name in the metadata author fields and there is no need for them to sign into the repository. In a nutshell: You invite contributors by giving them the repository link, and they sign in to create an account, and then you request that we grant them access.\xa0The library cannot create accounts for them before they sign in. See Roles and Permissions in the Dataverse Platform Users Guide']
response:
To add collaborators who are **not** affiliated with ASU so that they can **manage** (i.e., submit, edit, or control permissions for) datasets in the ASU Research Data Repository (RDR), you’ll need to work around the current authentication rules. Here’s a step‑by‑step guide based on the library’s official FAQ:

---

## 1. Understand the default limitation
- **Only users with an asu.edu email address can create an RDR account** and therefore can directly submit or manage datasets.
- External collaborators can **view and download** any published Dataverse (collection), but they **cannot** log in to create, edit, or manage datasets on their own.

---

## 2. Options for giving external collaborators “manager‑level” access

### A. Use an **ASU Courtesy Affiliate (ASURITE) ID**
1. **Ask the ASU sponsor (you) to submit a request** for an affiliate ID for each external collaborator.  
2. Fill out the **ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request – Add or Renew form** (the exact URL is provided in the FAQ).  
3. Once the affiliate ID is issued, the collaborator receives an **ASURITE username** that works with ASU Single Sign‑On, letting them log in to the RDR just like any other ASU user.  
4. **Note:** Affiliate IDs are **valid for one year** and must be renewed by the sponsor when they expire.

### B. Have the external collaborator **send files to you** for upload
1. Set up a **secure sharing space** (e.g., Google Drive, Dropbox, Box) where the external partner can drop their raw data and documentation.  
2. After you receive the files, **upload them to the RDR on their behalf** using your own ASU account.  
3. You can then add the external person as a **co‑owner or contributor** (see the “add users or co‑owners” FAQ) so they appear in the metadata and have appropriate permissions.

### C. Leverage **API‑based or third‑party collaboration platforms**
- **Dataverse API:** You (or a developer) can script uploads and metadata edits via the API, using an API token tied to an ASU account. This lets you automate the process of adding external data without giving them a direct login.  
- **Research notebooks / collaboration tools:** Platforms such as **LabArchives** or the **Open Science Framework (OSF)** can serve as a shared workspace. You can connect OSF to Dataverse using an API token, allowing external collaborators to work in OSF while you push the final dataset to RDR.  

---

## 3. Adding the external collaborator as a co‑owner (once they have an ASU/affiliate account)

1. **Invite the collaborator** by sending them the repository link.  
2. They must **sign in for the first time** (using their ASU or affiliate credentials).  
3. After their first sign‑in, contact the **ASU Library repository administration team** (via the “Ask a Librarian” portal or the library help desk) and request that they **grant the appropriate permission level** (e.g., “Contributor,” “Editor,” or “Owner”).  
4. If you only need to list them as an author without giving management rights, simply add their name in the **metadata author fields**—no account is required.

---

## 4. Summary of steps you’ll likely follow

| Goal | How to achieve it |
|------|-------------------|
| **Give external partner full management rights** | Request an **ASU Courtesy Affiliate ID** for them, then add them as a co‑owner after they sign in. |
| **Allow external partner to contribute data without an account** | Have them upload files to a **secure shared folder** (Google Drive/Dropbox) and you upload to RDR; then add them as a co‑owner or author in the metadata. |
| **Automate or script contributions** | Use the **Dataverse API** or connect via **OSF/LabArchives** with an API token; you retain the ASU account while the external partner works in the external platform. |

---

## 5. Need more help?

If any part of this process is unclear, or you need the exact link to the **ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request – Add or Renew** form, please reach out through **Ask a Librarian** (https://askalibrarian.asu.edu) or contact the **ASU Library Data Services team**. They can walk you through the affiliate request and the permission‑granting workflow.
reference: With our current ASU Research Data Repository authentication process, only submitters with asu.edu emails can create an account. Anyone outside the university can view and access published collections (known as Dataverses), but cannot directly submit/deposit or manage the metadata. If you have members of your project outside of ASU, you can have them submit their datasets and documentation to a secure sharing space like Google Drive or Dropbox and then upload the files on their behalf. If more direct interaction is desired, ASU sponsors can request that collaborators get an “affiliate ID” (ASURITE) to access ASU systems. The ID expires after one year and has to be renewed by the ASU sponsor at that time. To sponsor and request an ASU ID for your collaborators, fill out the ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request - Add or Renew form . Additional workarounds for research teams may include using the Dataverse API , research notebooks like LabArchives , or the OSF as a collaboration platform, and connecting the OSF to Dataverse (which uses an API token). For information on adding co-owners, see our FAQ entry on adding users or co-owners to my datasets .

user_input: Does ASU offer any plagiarism checkers?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: Does ASU offer any plagiarism checkers?

FAQ Answer:
ASU currently uses Turnitin (TII) as part of the Canvas Classroom Management Software. Instructors - Turnitin must be activated for each assignment. Check ASU’s How to Enable Turnitin for Online Assignments information page! Students - Check your assignment in Canvas to see if Turnitin has been activated.', 'FAQ Question: What is plagiarism and how can I prevent it?

FAQ Answer:
Take a look at our library guide search results on Academic Integrity . It includes valuable information plus links to additional resources to help you! We also have tutorials about these topics: Academic Integrity Plagiarism Awareness']
response:
Yes—Arizona State University provides a plagiarism‑checking tool through **Turnitin**.  

**How it works**

* **Turnitin is built into Canvas**, the university’s classroom‑management platform.  
* **Instructors** must turn the Turnitin feature on for each individual assignment.  They can follow the university’s “How to Enable Turnitin for Online Assignments” guide (available on the ASU website) to set it up.  
* **Students** simply need to look at the assignment details in Canvas; if Turnitin has been activated, your submission will be automatically checked for similarity when you submit.

If you’re an instructor and need step‑by‑step instructions, or if you have any trouble locating the Turnitin option in Canvas, you can consult the “How to Enable Turnitin for Online Assignments” page or reach out through **Ask a Librarian** for additional help.
reference: ASU currently uses Turnitin (TII) as part of the Canvas Classroom Management Software. Instructors - Turnitin must be activated for each assignment. Check ASU’s How to Enable Turnitin for Online Assignments information page! Students - Check your assignment in Canvas to see if Turnitin has been activated.

user_input: Are there due dates or fees for interlibrary loan items? Can I renew ILL items?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: Are there due dates or fees for interlibrary loan items? Can I renew ILL items?

FAQ Answer:
Yes, interlibrary loan items have due dates and overdue fees.\xa0 These differ depending on the item type and library who is lending the item to you. Due dates For physical items, you can check the due date on the strap attached to the item, or through your ILLiad account .\xa0 The average loan period for most physical items is between 4 and 8 weeks. Electronically delivered items like articles and ebooks do not need to be returned.\xa0 Your access will automatically expire after 60 days. Overdue fees Most ILL items are free to request, receive, and return. For interlibrary loan items that are not returned by the due date, there is a \xa0there is a $5 per day late fee with a maximum overdue fee of $25, and a default $150.00 replacement fee.\xa0The replacement fee may be adjusted depending on the actual cost of the item billed by the library who owns the item.\xa0 We have a high overdue fee as many libraries will block us from requesting any materials when their items are returned late. Home Delivery items are shipped FedEx 2 Day, and include a FedEx Ground return label.\xa0 You are responsible for overdue, damaged, and lost items. Renewals Renewals are usually not available for interlibrary loan items.\xa0To have ILL staff negotiate an extension on a due date with the owning library, please contact us prior to the due date on the item. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill', 'FAQ Question: What items can I request through interlibrary loan? What can’t I request?

FAQ Answer:
You can request these types of items through interlibrary loan: Books Book chapters (up to 3 per book) Journal articles (up to 3 per journal issue) Government documents Theses and dissertations Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks These items are not available to request: Textbooks Items for personal/non-academic use Entire journal issues Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks There is no limit to the amount of requests you can submit.\xa0 However, we recommend that you only request materials you can use in a reasonable amount of time to avoid overdue fees.\xa0 Renewals are not usually available. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill']
response:
**Due dates**

- **Physical items** (books, book chapters, DVDs, CDs, etc.) have a set due date that you can see on the strap attached to the item or by logging into your ILLiad account.  
- The typical loan period for most physical items is **4 – 8 weeks**, depending on the lending library’s policies.  
- **Electronically delivered items** (journal articles, e‑books, etc.) do not need to be returned. Your access automatically expires after **60 days**.

**Overdue fees**

- Most ILL items are free to request, receive, and return, but if a physical item is not returned by its due date you will be charged **$5 per day**.  
- The daily charge caps at **$25 total** in overdue fees.  
- In addition, there is a **default replacement fee of $150** (the amount may be adjusted to reflect the actual cost of the item as billed by the owning library).  
- For Home‑Delivery items (shipped FedEx 2‑Day), a FedEx Ground return label is included, but you are still responsible for any overdue, damaged, or lost items.

**Renewals**

- **Renewals are generally not available** for interlibrary‑loan items.  
- If you need more time, you must have ILL staff **negotiate an extension with the owning library**. To do this, contact the ILL department **before the item’s due date**.

**How to get help**

- **Phone:** (480) 965‑3282 (Monday – Friday, 8 am – 5 pm)  
- **Email:** ASU‑ILL@asu.edu  
- **More information:** https://libguides.asu.edu/ill

user_input: I will be graduating soon. How will that affect my due dates for library materials?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: I will be graduating soon. How will that affect my due dates for library materials?

FAQ Answer:
Congratulations on your graduation from ASU! Due dates: Graduation will not affect the due dates of materials checked out on your library account, even if they are due after graduation. Just remember, once you graduate, you will not be able to renew your library items. Library fees/fines: Yes, delinquent fees for library materials may affect graduation . Library billing contacts, policies, appeals, etc.', "FAQ Question: When I tried to renew my library books, it gave me today as the due date. What's wrong?

FAQ Answer:
Please take another look! Your books could be due next year ! If you renewed your books today and it shows today as the due date: Perhaps our library system thinks today is your last day with ASU (as student, faculty or staff). Contact us! so we can check our records!"]
response:
Congratulations on your upcoming graduation! Here’s what you need to know about how graduation impacts the items you currently have checked out from the ASU Library:

1. **Due dates stay the same** – Any library materials you’ve already borrowed will keep the original due date that was set when you checked them out. This means that even if the due date falls after you’ve officially graduated, you are still expected to return the items by that date.

2. **No more renewals after graduation** – Once your ASU status changes to “graduated,” the system will no longer allow you to renew any of your checked‑out items. If you anticipate needing more time with a particular item, it’s a good idea to renew it **before** your graduation date.

3. **Delinquent fees can affect graduation** – Outstanding fines or fees for overdue or lost items may be reported to the university’s billing office and could potentially hold up the processing of your diploma. It’s best to settle any library balances before you graduate.

4. **What to do if you have questions** – If you notice any unusual due‑date behavior (for example, a due date that shows as today) or you need help confirming your renewal eligibility, contact the library directly. The staff can verify your account status and make sure everything is in order.

**Quick checklist before you graduate**

- **Check your current due dates** in the library portal and note any items that are due after your graduation date.  
- **Renew any items you’ll need longer** before your graduation day.  
- **Return or pay for any overdue items** to avoid fines that could impact your diploma.  
- **Verify your contact information** so the library can reach you if there are any issues.

If you run into any problems or have additional questions, feel free to reach out to the ASU Library via the “Ask a Librarian” service or visit a circulation desk. Good luck with your graduation!
reference: Congratulations on your graduation from ASU! Due dates: Graduation will not affect the due dates of materials checked out on your library account, even if they are due after graduation. Just remember, once you graduate, you will not be able to renew your library items. Library fees/fines: Yes, delinquent fees for library materials may affect graduation . Library billing contacts, policies, appeals, etc.

response:
If you discover that the item you received through Interlibrary Loan (ILL) isn’t what you requested, follow these steps so the lending library can correct the mistake quickly:

### 1. Identify the type of item you received  
- **Article (PDF, scanned copy, etc.)**  
- **Physical item (book, DVD, other loan material)**  

### 2. Take action based on the item type  

#### **For Articles**  
1. **Reply to the original ILL email notice** you received when the article was delivered.  
2. In your reply, clearly state that the wrong article was sent and include any details that will help the staff locate the correct item (title, author, journal, etc.).  
3. **Be sure to include the transaction number (TN#)** in your message – it’s the identifier for your request.  
4. **If you have already deleted the original email**, send a new message to **ASU‑ILL@asu.edu** with the same information (problem description + TN#).

#### **For Books or Other Physical Loans**  
1. **Return the item to the ASU Interlibrary Loan office as soon as possible.**  
2. When you return it, **inform the ILL staff of the problem** (e.g., “I received the wrong book; the title should be …”).  
3. You can also **call or email** the ILL department to report the issue:  
   - **Phone:** (480) 965‑3282  
   - **Email:** ASU‑ILL@asu.edu  
   - **Include the transaction number (TN#) and a brief description of the error** in your call or email.  

### 3. What happens next?  
- The incorrect item will be **sent back to the lending library** that originally supplied it.  
- Once the item is **checked in and returned to the owning library**, the ILL staff will **re‑order the correct material** for you.  
- **Note:** The correct item cannot be reordered until the wrong one has been returned and processed by the lending library.

### 4. Keep records  
- Save any email confirmations or reference numbers you receive after reporting the problem.  
- If you need to follow up, having the TN# and a copy of your correspondence will speed up the process.

---

**Need further help?**  
If you run into any difficulties or have additional questions, feel free to contact the Interlibrary Loan department directly (phone or email above) or submit a new request through the **Ask a Librarian** service. They’re happy to assist you!

If you've deleted the email already, send an email to ASU-ILL@asu.edu .  Be sure to include the TN# (transaction number). For books or other loans: Please return it to Interlibrary Loan as soon as possible and tell us the problem. You can also call or email us, including the TN# and the problem, at (480) 965-3282 or ASU-ILL@asu.edu . The wrong item will be returned to the lending library and the correct one will be reordered. Interlibrary Loan cannot reorder material until it has been checked in and returned to the owning library.

retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: How do I get technical help with ASU Library resources?

FAQ Answer:
Technical help (ASU Library) If you are experiencing technical problems with any library online resource, such as broken links, catalog errors, database* problems, etc. and your attempts to troubleshoot the issue have failed, You can request help at: https://lib.asu.edu/systems/help . Sign in with your ASURITE ID and password Fill out and submit\xa0the form One of our technical support staff will respond via email to you as soon as possible. *For "400 Bad Request: Request Header or Cookie Too Large" errors when trying to access an article or database , please try the fixes listed in that FAQ before submitting a ticket.', 'FAQ Question: How do I get technical help for non-library issues?

FAQ Answer:
For technical problems with\xa0university\xa0resources such as the ASU\xa0wireless network, connectivity issues, and password problems, start at the main ASU Contact page to get to the correct unit to assist you.']
response:
If you run into technical problems with any ASU Library online resource—such as broken links, catalog errors, database issues, or similar errors—follow these steps to get help:

1. **Visit the Library’s technical‑support portal**  
   Go to: **https://lib.asu.edu/systems/help**

2. **Sign in**  
   Log in with your **ASURITE ID** and password. This lets the support team see your campus affiliation and route your request appropriately.

3. **Submit a help request**  
   - Fill out the online form with as much detail as possible (what you were trying to access, the exact error message, the browser you’re using, etc.).  
   - Submit the form.

4. **Wait for a response**  
   A member of the Library’s technical support staff will review your ticket and reply to you via email as soon as possible.

### Quick tip for a common error
If you see a **“400 Bad Request: Request Header or Cookie Too Large”** message when trying to open an article or database, try the troubleshooting steps listed in the Library’s FAQ for that error **before** submitting a ticket. Resolving the cookie/header issue often fixes the problem without needing further assistance.

If you need help with non‑library university resources (e.g., ASU wireless, password resets, etc.), start at the main ASU Contact page to be directed to the appropriate support unit.

response:
Sure thing! If you need to cancel a request you placed for a book (or any other item) from another ASU library, just follow these steps:

1. **Go to the ASU Library home page**  
   Open your web browser and navigate to [https://lib.asu.edu](https://lib.asu.edu).

2. **Access your account**  
   - In the black header at the top of the page, hover over **“Accounts.”**  
   - From the drop‑down menu, click **“My Library Account.”**  

3. **Sign in**  
   - If you aren’t already signed in, you’ll be prompted to log in with your **ASURITE ID** and password.  

4. **View your holds**  
   - Once you’re in your account, click on **“View Holds.”** This will show a list of all the items you’ve requested (holds) from ASU libraries.

5. **Cancel the unwanted request**  
   - Locate the item you no longer want.  
   - Check the box next to that item (or use the cancel button/link next to it).  
   - Confirm the cancellation when prompted.

6. **Alternative: Cancel by phone**  
   - If you’d rather not do it online, you can call the ASU Library and ask a staff member to cancel the request for you.  
   - **Phone:** 480‑965‑3605  

That’s it! Once you’ve cancelled, the request will be removed from the queue, and the item will be released for other patrons. If you run into any trouble or have additional questions, feel free to call the library or use the **Ask a Librarian** service for further assistance.
reference: Want to cancel your request? user_input: Where can I find a permanent link to an item I found in One Search?
retrieved_contexts:
['FAQ Question: Where can I find a permanent link to an item I found in One Search?

FAQ Answer:
To find the persistent URL or permalink for a record you found in One Search... In the short display The short display is usually part of your results list as a numbered item (such as the number 3 item in your results list). Along the top right of the short display for the record, look for and click the three dots icon (...). In the resulting list of action options, click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use . In the full record display From the short record in the results list, click the title of the item until you find the longer full record for the item.\xa0 If there were multiple formats listed in the short record, you may need to click on the title in the new numbered list of short records, and then click on the title of an item to get to the full record! Look for the "Send to" section of the record to find the permalink. Then click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use.', 'FAQ Question: How do I find an item if I already have the full citation - author, title, etc.?

FAQ Answer:
There are several ways to see if we have the article, book, or other item you need. Use our Citation Linker . Remember to select the appropriate option on the form for: Article\xa0(includes DOI search field) Book Journal\xa0(includes DOI search field) Search by DOI with the DOI (Digital Object Identifier) Resolver Search by the citation information in ASU Library's One Search * Place quotation marks around the article title so that the words are searched as that particular phrase. Example: " University of transformation: Michael Crow at Arizona State University "']
response:
Sure! If you’ve located a record in **One Search** and need a permanent URL (also called a *permalink*) that you can save or share, you can get it in either the short display (the brief entry in your results list) or the full record view. Here’s how to do both:

---

### 1. From the **short display** (the brief entry in your results list)

1. **Locate the short display** for the item you want. This is the numbered entry that appears in the list of results (e.g., the “#3” item).  
2. In the **top‑right corner** of that short display, you’ll see a **three‑dots icon (⋯)**.  
3. Click the three‑dots icon. A menu of action options will appear.  
4. Choose **“permalink.”**  
5. A URL will be displayed—simply **copy** that link and paste it wherever you need (e.g., in a citation, email, or personal notes). This URL is persistent and will always bring you back to that exact record.

---

### 2. From the **full record display** (the detailed view of the item)

1. From the short display, **click the title** of the item to open the longer, full record.  
   - *Note:* If the short display lists multiple formats (e.g., a book and an e‑book), you may need to click the title again in the new numbered list to reach the full record for the specific format you want.  
2. Once you’re on the full record page, scroll down to the **“Send to”** section.  
3. In that section, look for the **“permalink”** option and click it.  
4. A permanent URL will appear—**copy** it for future use.

---

### Quick Tips

- **Use the permalink** whenever you need a stable link that won’t change over time. It’s especially handy for sharing resources with classmates, adding to research notes, or embedding in a bibliography.  
- If you ever lose the link, you can always return to One Search, locate the item again, and repeat the steps above.  

---

If you run into any trouble or have additional questions about using One Search, feel free to ask or visit the **Ask a Librarian** service for personalized help. Happy researching!
reference: To find the persistent URL or permalink for a record you found in One Search... In the short display The short display is usually part of your results list as a numbered item (such as the number 3 item in your results list). Along the top right of the short display for the record, look for and click the three dots icon (...). In the resulting list of action options, click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use . In the full record display From the short record in the results list, click the title of the item until you find the longer full record for the item.  If there were multiple formats listed in the short record, you may need to click on the title in the new numbered list of short records, and then click on the title of an item to get to the full record! Look for the "Send to" section of the record to find the permalink. Then click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use.
